label: Uneasy calm
summary: The world spent Independence Day in the same uneasy-calm band it has held all week, with grief and heat pressing on one side and quiet scientific and diplomatic progress on the other. Iran opened a seven-day state funeral for slain former supreme leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, killed with several relatives in the February 28 US-Israel strikes; Tehran's Imam Khomeini Mosalla began a 24-hour farewell as officials forecast 15 to 20 million mourners and delegations from more than 100 countries, and Washington agreed to pause indirect denuclearization talks for the week, a fragile diplomacy that reads as pause rather than progress. Europe's deadliest conflict since 1945 ground on as Kyiv absorbed the aftermath of its worst strike of the year, Russian-installed authorities declared a state of emergency in Crimea under intensified Ukrainian drone raids, and Moscow moved to cancel most Kerch Bridge rail routes from July 8. In Sudan, UN human rights chief Volker Turk warned of an unfolding catastrophe in besieged Al Obeid, where roughly half a million people face killings, abductions and sexual violence and a full assault could displace hundreds of thousands more. The humanitarian ledger stayed heavy: Venezuela's twin-quake toll climbed toward 2,595 dead with nearly 50,000 unaccounted for, and the DR Congo Bundibugyo Ebola epidemic reached more than 1,400 cases and 438 deaths. Climate weighed on both hemispheres, with a heat dome placing over 160 million Americans under alerts and straining power grids through the July 4 weekend while European heatwaves left Spain with more than 1,000 heat-related excess deaths in June. Against that ran genuine lift: India and Japan sealed a summit pact deepening AI, semiconductor, shipbuilding and defence ties; the WHO enrolled the first patient in a Bundibugyo Ebola treatment trial; researchers reported CAR-T therapy eliminating glioblastoma in preclinical models and a spray-on powder that halts severe bleeding in about a second; and the Dow had closed at a record two days earlier as rate-hike fears eased after a soft jobs report. The headline holds at 44, a notch below the neutral anchor, weighed by war, siege and heat even as science and public health quietly delivered.

drivers.headline: Iran opens a seven-day state funeral for slain former supreme leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, with Tehran's Imam Khomeini Mosalla beginning a 24-hour farewell and officials forecasting 15 to 20 million mourners and delegations from more than 100 countries, as the United States agrees to pause indirect denuclearization talks for the week so negotiators can attend, a fragile diplomatic breathing space rather than a breakthrough

Russian-installed authorities declare a state of emergency in Crimea as intensified Ukrainian drone and missile strikes cause widespread disruption, with Moscow announcing that from July 8 some 11 train routes over the Kerch Bridge will be cancelled, leaving only seven daily services, as the aftermath of Russia's deadliest strike on Kyiv this year still weighs on the capital

UN human rights chief Volker Turk warns that a human rights catastrophe is unfolding in Sudan's besieged city of Al Obeid, where roughly half a million people face killings, abductions, torture and sexual violence between the army and the Rapid Support Forces, with at least 45 civilians killed in drone strikes between June 6 and 28 and fears that a full assault could displace hundreds of thousands more, echoing last year's atrocities in El Fasher

Venezuela's twin-earthquake death toll climbs toward 2,595 with more than 12,400 injured and nearly 50,000 people still unaccounted for, as acting President Delcy Rodriguez reports more than 860 aftershocks and the UN's migration agency estimates up to 6.8 million people could need shelter, water, sanitation and healthcare after the back-to-back magnitude-7.2 and 7.5 quakes near Caracas and La Guaira

A dangerous heat dome traps more than half the United States through the July 4 weekend, placing over 160 million Americans under heat alerts with temperatures up to 105 degrees Fahrenheit, threatening to overwhelm power grids and forcing Amtrak to slow trains in the Northeast, Southeast and Midwest, in what could be the hottest Independence Day on record for millions

Europe's record-breaking summer heat presses on after Spain attributed more than 1,000 excess deaths to its second-hottest June ever and Portugal faced 42-degree-Celsius highs with very high wildfire risk, part of a continent-wide run of broken temperature records that World Weather Attribution scientists say would have been virtually impossible without climate change

India and Japan seal a wide-ranging pact at the 16th annual summit between Prime Ministers Narendra Modi and Sanae Takaichi, signing agreements to deepen cooperation on artificial intelligence, semiconductors, shipbuilding, critical minerals, defence and maritime security and adopting a joint economic-security roadmap, as bilateral trade reaches 27.5 billion dollars and Beijing cautions the partnership should not target third parties

The WHO enrolls the first patient in a clinical trial of experimental treatments for the DR Congo Bundibugyo Ebola outbreak, testing Mapp Biopharmaceutical's MBP134 antibody therapy alone and combined with Gilead's remdesivir, as treatment capacity expands to 650 beds even as the epidemic reaches more than 1,400 cases and 438 deaths and community mistrust and violence keep hampering the response

US markets close out the holiday week on solid footing, with the Dow having settled at an all-time high on July 2 and stocks rising after a soft June jobs report showing just 57,000 positions added quieted expectations of a near-term Federal Reserve rate hike, while the small-cap Russell 2000 booked its best first half since 1991 with a 22 percent gain

A new study finds CAR-T cell therapy could one day target glioblastoma, the deadliest brain cancer, after the engineered immune cells eliminated detectable tumours in preclinical models, one of several medical advances this week alongside a spray-on powder developed by KAIST that can stop life-threatening bleeding in about one second by instantly forming a strong gel over a wound

Australia and Vanuatu sign the Nakamal Agreement, a treaty elevating their partnership and formalizing Australia as Vanuatu's primary security and development partner, while Canberra pledges a 2 million dollar contribution toward emergency food, shelter, water and psychosocial support for Venezuela's earthquake survivors

The Our Ocean Conference closes with more than 6.4 billion dollars pledged to protect corals, coasts and whales, and fresh European data shows air pollution falling across the continent with sulphur and nitrogen oxide emissions down 3 to 5 percent a year, amid a run of environmental wins reported this week

sources_consulted: Wikipedia (Timeline of the Russo-Ukrainian war / State funeral of Ali Khamenei / 2026 Venezuela earthquakes / 2026 Ebola epidemic / 2026 European heatwaves / Gaza peace plan)
